## TOWARDS A STATISTICAL THEORY OF DATA SELECTION UNDER WEAK SUPERVISION

## **Germain Kolossov, Andrea Montanari, Pulkit Tandon**

Granica Computing, Inc. _{_ germain.kolossov,andrea.montanari,pulkit.tandon _}_ @granica.ai

## ABSTRACT

Given a sample of size _N_ , it is often useful to select a subsample of smaller size _n < N_ to be used for statistical estimation or learning. Such a data selection step is useful to reduce the requirements of data labeling and the computational complexity of learning. We assume to be given _N_ unlabeled samples _{_ _**x** i}i≤N_ , and to be given access to a ‘surrogate model’ that can predict labels _yi_ better than random guessing. Our goal is to select a subset of the samples, to be denoted by _{_ _**x** i}i∈G_ , of size _|G|_ = _n < N_ . We then acquire labels for this set and we use them to train a model via regularized empirical risk minimization. By using a mixture of numerical experiments on real and synthetic data, and mathematical derivations under low- and high- dimensional asymptotics, we show that: ( _i_ ) Data selection can be very effective, in particular beating training on the full sample in some cases; ( _ii_ ) Certain popular choices in data selection methods (e.g. unbiased reweighted subsampling, or influence function-based subsampling) can be substantially suboptimal.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Labeling is a notoriously laborious task in machine learning. A possible approach towards reducing this burden is to select a small subset of training samples that are most valuable for training.

Data selection-based learning consists of two steps – ( _i_ ) _Data Selection_ : given feature vectors _**X**_ := ( _**x** i_ ) _i≤N_ , select a subset _G ⊆_ [ _N_ ] of size _n_ (or close to _n_ ); ( _ii_ ) _Training_ : having acquired labels for the selected subset _{yi}i∈G_ , train a model _f_ ( _·_ ; _**θ**_ ) : R _[p] →_ R (with parameters _**θ**_ ) on the labeled data _{_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _}i∈G_ . Throughout this paper we will focus on methods with the following structure. In the first step, set _G_ is generated by selecting each datapoint _i_ independently with probability _πi_ = _π_ ( _**x** i_ ). Namely

The second step (training) is carried out by (weighted) empirical risk minimization (ERM) over the selected set _G_ . Namely, given loss function _ℓ_ : R _×_ R _→_ R and regularizer Ω: R _[p] →_ R, we solve

The weights _wi_ can depend on the feature vectors, and are designed as to reduce the error. A popular choice is _wi_ = cst _/πi_ because the resulting _R_[ˆ] _N_ ( _**θ**_ ) is an unbiased version of the full empirical risk.
Throughout this paper, we will assume data ( _**x** i, yi_ ) to be i.i.d. samples from a common distribution P _∈ P_ (R _[d] ×_ R) and will evaluate a data selection procedure via the test error:

Unlike the most common active learning scenario, we crucially assume to be given a fixed data sample _{_ _**x** i}i≤N_ (without labels) and carry out a single data selection step, often referred as ‘Pool-based active learning’ (Settles, 2012).

Before summarizing our contributions, it is useful to mention some of the existing approaches.

**Bayesian methods.** Within a Bayesian setting, to select the subsample _G_ is to minimize the conditional entropy of _**θ**_ given the data Lindley (1956); Seung et al. (1992). This is a first example of “uncertainty sampling.” We refer to Houlsby et al. (2011); Gal et al. (2017) for recent pointers, with a focus on online active learning. See Cui et al. (2021) for a recent exception.

**Heuristic approaches.** Several groups developed measures of the impact of each sample on the model: Lewis & Gale (1994) use probabilities predicted by a single current model; Vodrahalli et al. (2018) use the norm of _∇_ _**θ** ℓ_ ( _yi, f_ ( _**x** i,_ _**θ**_ )); Jiang et al. (2019) use the loss itself _ℓ_ ( _yi, f_ ( _**x** i,_ _**θ**_ )).

**Leverage scores** have been used for a long time in statistics as a measure of importance of a data point _i ∈{_ 1 _, . . . , N }_ in linear regression (Chatterjee & Hadi, 1986). Recall that the leverage of sample _**x** i_ is defined as _Hii_ := _**x**_[T] _i_[(] _**[X]**_[T] _**[X]**_[)] _[−]_[1] _**[x]**[i]_[.][Their][study][was][reinvigorated][by][a][numerical] linear algebra perspective (Drineas & Mahoney, 2006; Drineas et al., 2011). Statistical analyses of leverage score-based data selection was developed in Ma et al. (2014); Raskutti & Mahoney (2016); Ma et al. (2022). These works all assume _wi_ = 1 _/πi_ for unbiasedness.

**Influence functions.** Let _**θ** ∗_ = arg min _**θ** ∈_ R _pR_ ( _**θ**_ ) denote the population minimizer. For M- estimators (1.2) (under regularity conditions) approximate linearity holds as _n →∞_ for _p_ fixed (van der Vaart, 2000)

where _ψ_ : R _[p] ×_ R _→_ R is the so-called influence function. This approximation can be used to select the probabilities _πi_ and weights _wi_ . Several authors have developed this approach in the context of generalized linear models, while assuming unbiasedness _wi_ = 1 _/πi_ , resulting in the choice _πi ∝∥ψ_ ( _**x** i, yi_ ) _∥_ 2 (Ting & Brochu, 2018; Wang et al., 2018; Ai et al., 2021). Wang et al. (2020) shows potential for improvement by using biased schemes. These works assume a specific asymptotics in which 1 _≪ n ≪ N_ . In many applications, we would like the generalization error achieved from the _n_ -subsample to be comparable to the one with full sample and hence focus on the case _n_ = _γN_ , _γ_ = Θ(1).

**Margin-based selection.** In a recent paper Sorscher et al. (2022) show that, in the case of the binary perceptron, under a noiseless teacher-student distribution, selecting samples far from the margin is beneficial in a high-dimensional or data-poor regime. This is in stark contrast with influencefunction and related approaches that upsample data points whose labels are more difficult to predict. While our results confirm these findings, measuring uncertainty in terms of distance from the margin is somewhat specific to binary classification under certain distributional assumptions[1] .

## 2 DEFINITIONS

Since our objective is to alleviate the need to label data, we will focus on data selection schemes that do not use the labels ( _yi_ ) _i≤N_ . On the other hand, we will assume to have access to a surrogate model Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) which is a probability kernel from R _[d]_ to R. We will consider two settings – ( _i_ ) Ideal surrogate: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) = P(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is the actual conditional distribution of the labels. Of course this is the very object we want to learn. Since learning scheme is constrained, the problem is nevertheless non-trivial and informative. ( _ii_ ) Imperfect surrogates: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is predictive of the actual value of the labels, but does not coincide with the Bayes predictor.

The selection probabilities _πi_ and weights _wi_ can depend on _**x** i_ and Psu( _· |_ _**x** i_ ) (the conditional distribution of _yi_ given _**x** i_ under the surrogate). We will omit the dependence of the surrogate predictions unless needed, and write _πi_ = _π_ ( _**x** i_ ), _wi_ = _w_ ( _**x** i_ ).

It is convenient to encode a general data selection process into random variables _Si_ ( _**x** i_ ) _≥_ 0 which depend on _**x** i,_ Psu( _· |_ _**x** i_ ), and also on additional randomness independent across different samples[2] . We recover the original formulation by setting _Si_ ( _**x** i_ ) = _w_ ( _**x** i_ ) **1** _i∈G_ , P( _i ∈ G|_ _**X** ,_ _**y**_ ) = _π_ ( _**x** i_ ). **Definition 2.1.** _A data selection scheme is_ unbiased _if_ E _{S_ ( _**x**_ ) _|_ _**X** ,_ _**y** }_ = 1 _. We denote the set of unbiased data selection schemes by U ._

Throughout, we consider _n, N →∞_ , with converging ratio _n/N → γ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) . We refer to _γ_ as to the ‘subsampling fraction.’ We are interested in _γ_ bounded away from 0 because we want to keep the accuracy close to the full sample accuracy. Standard notations are summarized in Appendix A.

## 3 SUMMARY OF RESULTS

Our theory is based on two types of asymptotics, that capture complementary regimes. In Section 4 we will study the low-dimensional asymptotics, whereby _p_ is fixed as _n, N →∞_ . This analysis applies to fairly general data distributions and models _f_ ( _·_ ; _**θ**_ ). In Section 5, we will instead assume _p →∞_ as _n, N →∞_ , with _N/p → δ_ 0 _∈_ (0 _, ∞_ ). In this case we study convex M-estimators, for models that are linear in the features _**x** i_ . Namely, _L_ ( _**θ**_ ; _yi,_ _**x** i_ ) = _L∗_ ( _⟨_ _**θ** ,_ _**x** i⟩_ ; _yi_ ), with _L∗_ convex in its first argument, and _**x** i_ standard Gaussian. We next summarize some of the insights from our work and illustrate them in Figure 1 (experiments with real data, described in Section 7).

**Unbiased subsampling can be suboptimal.** As mentioned in the introduction, the majority of theoretical studies assumes unbiased subsampling: _wi ∝_ 1 _/πi_ . We show both theoretically and empirically that this can lead to potentially unbounded loss in accuracy with respect to biased schemes.

Low-dimensional asymptotics provide a compelling explanation: estimation error is inversely proportional to the curvature of the expected risk. Unbiased methods do not change the curvature, while biased methods can increase the curvature. In fact, we will prove that unbiased methods are suboptimal under a broad set of conditions. This result is illustrated in Figure 1. In particular, in Figure 1 we compare a biased and an unbiased scheme (with selection probabilities which approximate the influence-function scheme).

**Optimal selection depends on the subsampling fraction.** Popular data selection methods are independent of subsampling fraction in the sense that the selection probabilities _πi_ can be computed without knowing the target sampling fraction _n/N_ , except from the overall normalization that enforces the constraint (2.2). In contrast, we will see that optimal schemes depend in a non-trivial way on _n/N_ . This is very natural: the utility of sample ( _**x** i, yi_ ) depends on the other selected samples.

**Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection.** How to proceed in absence of the labels _yi_ ? A natural idea would be to start from selection probabilities that use the labels, _π_ 0( _yi,_ _**x** i_ ), and then take a conditional expectation with respect to _yi_ , under the surrogate model: _π_ ( _**x** i_ ) = Esu _{π_ 0( _yi,_ _**x** i_ ) _|_ _**x** i}_ . We show that this is not always optimal, and in particular better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection (if used in a plugin fashion as described).

**Uncertainty-based subsampling is effective.** The simplest rule of thumb emerging from our work confirms earlier research: subsampling schemes based on how uncertain surrogate predictions are, often perform well. On the other hand, influence-based subsampling is not generally optimal. First: in low-dimension, stronger bias towards hard examples is beneficial, cf. Section 4.2. Second: in certain high-dimensional cases, this bias must be reversed, and ‘easier’ examples should be selected, see Section 5 (in agreement with Sorscher et al. (2022)). Third, adding nuance to the previous point, selecting the ‘hardest’ or ‘easiest’ even in high dimension, is not always optimal, see e.g. Section 6.

**Data subsampling can beat full-sample training.** Our objective is to select a fraction _γ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) of the data such that, training on the selected subset yields test error close to training on the whole dataset. To our surprise, see Figure 1, we discover that good data selection can actually reduce the test error below the one obtained from the full sample for _γ_ as small as 0 _._ 4. A naive explanation would be that information is being passed from the surrogate model to the trained model via data selection. While this is of course true, the effect is more subtle. Indeed, we achieve a reduction in test error even when the surrogate model is trained on significantly less than (1 _− γ_ ) _N_ random samples (so that overall, we are using significantly less than _N_ labels). Recent empirical studies (Nakkiran et al., 2021; Guo et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2022; Sorscher et al., 2022; Gadre et al., 2024) have observed similar phenomenon for deep models with real data but we show that this is true even for simpler settings.

## 4.2 BIASED DATA SELECTION

We now consider biased subsampling schemes _S_ . It is straightforward to see that the weights can always be chosen to be deterministic (see Appendix E). In other words, _S_ ( _**x**_ ) = _w_ ( _**x**_ ) with probability _π_ ( _**x**_ ), and _S_ ( _**x**_ ) = 0 otherwise, for two deterministic functions _w, π_ . We can identify these data selection scheme with the pair ( _π, w_ ), hence we will also write _ρ_ ( _π, w_ ; _**Q**_ ) for _ρ_ ( _S_ ; _**Q**_ ).

Among these, we focus on ‘non-reweighting schemes’ that are defined by the additional condition _w_ = 1 and denote them by _N_ . Non-reweighting schemes are practically convenient since they do not require to modify the training procedure. Optimal non-reweighting schemes have a simple structure, as stated below. ## 4.3 DATA SELECTION CAN BEAT FULL-SAMPLE ESTIMATION

One striking phenomenon illustrated in Figure 1 is that ERM with respect to a selected subset of _n < N_ samples out of _N_ total produces a better model than ERM on the full dataset. This is the case even when the surrogate model is trained on _n_ su samples, with _n_ su + _n_ is significantly below _N_ .

Is the non-monotonic behavior compatible with theoretical expectations? The answer depends on the data selection scheme (see Appendix H for details and an illustration): ( _i_ ) Unbiased data selection is always monotone, cf. Section 4.1; ( _ii_ ) The optimal data selection scheme with reweighting is also monotone by a simple reduction argument. However, for _n_ = _N_ , this scheme does not reduce to unweighted ERM, but to optimally weighted ERM. ( _iii_ ) Finally, non-monotonicity is possible in a neighborhood of _γ_ = 1 for non-reweighting schemes, as established below (see Appendix I).

We will next prove that, in a natural setting, unbiased subsampling is always suboptimal below a certain subsampling ratio. (The last condition is likely to be a proof artifact, cf. Appendix J.) We show in Appendix J that the conditions below hold generically for generalized linear models.

## 4.5 IMPERFECT SURROGATES

In a more realistic setting, the surrogate model Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) does not coincide with the actual conditional distribution of _yi_ given _**x** i_ . We model this situation using a minimax point of view. Namely, we assume that the actual conditional distribution is in a neighborhood of the surrogate model, and study the worst case error in this neighborhood. The minimax theorem then implies that we should use data selection schemes that are optimal for the worst data distribution in this neighborhood. We will not pursue the construction of minimax optimal data selection schemes in this paper. However, we point out that the broad conclusion is consistent with some of our empirical findings in Section 7. Namely, in certain cases using a less accurate surrogate model yields better data selection. Theorems and examples are presented in Appendix K.

## 5 HIGH-DIMENSIONAL ASYMPTOTICS: GENERALIZED LINEAR MODELS

We next study a high dimensional setting whereby the number of samples _N_ and the dimension _p_ diverge simultaneously. More precisely, we assume _n, N, p →∞_ with _n/N → γ_ , _N/p → δ_ 0 for some _γ, δ_ 0 _∈_ (0 _, ∞_ ). We will restrict ourselves to the case of (potentially misspecified) generalized linear models already introduced in Example 4.2. As we will see in this section and the next, the high-dimensional setting allow us to unveil a few interesting phenomena, namely: ( _i_ ) Biasing data selection towards hard samples (those that are uncertain under the surrogate model) can be suboptimal; ( _ii_ ) Even when biasing towards hard samples is effective, selecting the top hardest one can lead to poor behavior at small _γ_ ; ( _iii_ ) A one parameter family of selection probabilities introduced in the next section is broadly effective.

## 7 NUMERICAL RESULTS: REAL DATA

For our real-data experiments we used an Autonomous Vehicle (AV) dataset and a binary classification task. As for synthetic data, we use ridge regularized logistic regression and study data selection under the one-parameter scheme of Eq. (6.1). This model was trained and tested on unsupervised features extracted from image data. Appendix O provides the details of the dataset and experiments, and further empirical results supporting the claims.

Figure 1 (left) shows test error for optimal _λ_ and _α_ = 0 _._ 5 fixed, for unbiased and non-reweighted (biased) subsampling, using ‘weak’ and ‘strong’ surrogate models. Note that: ( _i_ ) Restricting to ‘unbiased’ data selection is unnecessary and sometimes harmful; ( _ii_ ) The efficacy of weakly supervised data-selection methods is not highly sensitive to the quality of the surrogate (see also Figure 9 in Appendix O).

Figure 1 (right) shows test error for the optimal parameters _{λ, α,_ surr _,_ bias _}_ , selected by minimizing the misclassification rate on the validation set. The reported results are computed on the test set. We compare this optimal choice by a ‘constant strategy’ that uses _λ_ = 0 _._ 01, _α_ = 0 _._ 5, non-reweighted subsampling and weak surrogate models. This constant strategy performs almost optimally when _N_ is large, and still provides consistent improvements over random subsampling when _N_ is smaller. This strategy reflects influence-function based subsampling, although without reweighting and using a weak surrogate model. This figure highlights that: ( _i_ ) Selection criteria based on the ‘uncertainty’ associated to the label of a data point are effective, however influence function can be suboptimal; ( _ii_ ) Learning after data selection can outperform learning on the full sample.

progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.label: Progressive (gemini-3-flash-preview)
progressive__gemini-3-flash-preview.overall_feedback:
This paper addresses a timely and practically significant problem: how to theoretically ground data selection strategies when one has access to a noisy surrogate model. The quality of the work is high, characterized by a rigorous blend of high-dimensional statistical theory and empirical validation. Its primary clarity lies in the well-defined two-step framework (selection via independent Bernoulli sampling followed by weighted ERM) and the explicit distinction between biased and unbiased reweighting—a nuanced point often overlooked in heuristic-driven literature. The most significant contribution is the counter-intuitive finding that biased selection can outperform both unbiased sampling and, in certain regimes, training on the "full" dataset.

However, a significant issue is the potential sensitivity of the theoretical results to the quality of the "surrogate model." While the authors classify surrogates as "strong" or "weak," the formal relationship between the surrogate's error distribution and the downstream model's generalization remains somewhat opaque in the introduction. Furthermore, while the high-dimensional asymptotic regime ($n = \gamma N, p/n \to \alpha$) provides elegant closed-form insights, it may not immediately capture the dynamics of modern overparameterized regimes (where $p \gg n$) common in deep learning, unless the feature embeddings (like the SwAV embeddings used in Figure 1) are assumed to be fixed and linear. The paper would be strengthened by explicitly defining the surrogate model's noise structure to determine exactly when the "biased" strategy’s variance reduction outweighs its introduced bias.

comments.title: Mathematical Error and Inconsistency in Score Function Z(x; π)
comments.quote: Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi) := - \text{Tr}\left( \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{Q H}_{\pi}^{-1} \right) + 2\text{Tr}\left( \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{Q H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{G}_{\pi} \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \right)
comments.explanation: There is a structural and sign inconsistency in the definition of the optimal score function $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi)$ in Section 4.2. Deriving the stationarity condition for minimizing the error $\rho(\pi)$ involves the derivative of $\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$, which is $-\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$. The resulting score should represent the influence of a single point on the objective; however, the signs in Eq (4.9) appear reversed relative to the decision rule $\pi = \mathbb{1}_{\{Z \ge \lambda\}}$. If $Z$ represents the 'value' of a sample to be maximized, the gradient covariance $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ (noise) should typically have a positive coefficient to be avoided/minimized or specific weighting to be selected. As written, the negative sign on the first term implies that high-noise samples are penalized in a way that might contradict the 'hard-example' selection narrative later in the text. Furthermore, there is a potential redundant scaling factor of $\gamma^{-1}$ in the definition of $\rho_{nr}$ which would lead to an incorrect $\gamma^{-2}$ variance scaling.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Better surrogates lead to worse selection' claim
comments.quote: Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection. How to proceed in absence of the labels $y_i$ ? ... We show that this is not always optimal.
comments.explanation: The paper makes the counter-intuitive claim that more accurate surrogate models (closer to the true $P(y|x)$) can result in higher generalization error when used in a 'plug-in' fashion. While the authors qualify this by noting the suboptimality of the plug-in strategy itself, the methodological implications are underspecified. It is unclear whether 'better' refers to the predictive calibration of the surrogate or its rank-correlation with the true influence scores. Without clarifying if the bias of a 'weak' surrogate coincidentally aligns with the optimal biased sampling rule, the claim remains a paradox that could confuse practitioners attempting to select surrogate models.

Additionally, the definition of the surrogate model in the experimental section appears corrupted by OCR, stating 'f: su ... \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \boldsymbol{\theta} 0', which is syntactically broken. This makes it impossible to verify the functional form of the selection probabilities used in the simulations or to distinguish the surrogate model from the ground truth parameters.

The provided expression involves a product of these terms that does not obviously simplify to the standard 'square root of the second moment' of the influence function. Additionally, the paper uses the symbol $Z(\mathbf{x})$ to denote the score for both unbiased (Section 4.1) and biased (Section 4.2) sampling, despite them representing fundamentally different quantities (a norm vs. a trace-based derivative). This overlapping notation complicates the transition between the two regimes.

comments.title: Data-Generating Process Mismatch in Synthetic Experiments
comments.explanation: The synthetic data description for Figure 2 contains logical inconsistencies. It lists $P(Y=1|z)$ as a constant 0.1, which would imply the labels are independent of the features, rendering 'uncertainty' or 'influence' sampling logic irrelevant. This is likely a typo for a noise parameter (like $\eta$) or a model coefficient. Furthermore, the implementation uses an empirical sum constraint $\sum \pi_i = n$ for simulations, which introduces dependencies between samples that technically violate the i.i.d. assumptions used in the theoretical proofs.

The quality of the work is high, characterized by a rigorous blend of high-dimensional statistical theory and empirical validation. The most significant contribution is the counter-intuitive finding that biased selection can outperform both unbiased sampling and, in certain regimes, training on the "full" dataset.

However, a significant issue is the potential sensitivity of the theoretical results to the quality of the "surrogate model." While the authors classify surrogates as "strong" or "weak," the formal relationship between the surrogate's error distribution and the downstream model's generalization remains somewhat opaque in the introduction. The paper would be strengthened by explicitly defining the surrogate model's noise structure to determine exactly when the "biased" strategy’s variance reduction outweighs its introduced bias.

comments.title: Conflicting definition of the scaling factor γ
comments.quote: Specifically, the paper states on page 3: 'Throughout, we consider $n, N \to \infty$, with converging ratio $n/N \to \gamma \in (0, 1)$ . We refer to $\gamma$ as to the ‘subsampling fraction.’' However, Equation (2.2) defines the constraint as: $\sum_{i=1}^N \pi_i = n$.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, $\gamma$ is defined as the ratio $n/N$. However, earlier in the 'Notation & Definitions' and on page 3, the paper notes: 'We will enforce that the target sample size $n$ is achieved in expectation, namely $\sum_{i=i}^N \pi_i = n$'. When the text moves to the 'Summary of Results' and 'Low-Dimensional Asymptotics' sections, it refers to $\gamma = \Theta(1)$. In Proposition 4.1 and Equation (4.10), $\gamma$ is used as the target for the expectation of $\pi(x)$, i.e., $E[\pi(x)] = \gamma$. If $n = \sum \pi_i$, then $E[\pi] = n/N$. This matches the definition $\gamma = n/N$. However, in the 'Summary of Results' page 4, the text states: 'good data selection can actually reduce the test error below the one obtained from the full sample for $\gamma$ as small as 0.4.' If $\gamma$ is the subsampling fraction, the full sample corresponds to $\gamma = 1$. The notation is mostly consistent, but the reader must be careful to distinguish between the 'subsampling fraction' $\gamma$ and the 'sampling probability' $\pi_i$. The paper uses $\gamma$ to denote the budget $n/N$. In Section 4.2 (Equation 4.10), it says $\lambda$ is chosen so $E[\pi_{nr}(x)] = \gamma$. This is mathematically sound, but because $\pi_i$ is a probability ($\le 1$), this implies $\gamma$ must be $\le 1$, which is consistent with the definition $n < N$.

Under the 'Ideal' or 'Unbiased' case, $E[S|\mathbf{x}] = 1$, which implies $R_S(\boldsymbol{\theta}) = R(\boldsymbol{\theta})$ and thus $\mathbf{H}_S = \mathbf{H}$ (the population Hessian). However, for 'Biased' schemes (Section 4.2), the weights $w_i$ do not necessarily satisfy $E[S|\mathbf{x}] = 1$. While the text mentions that $\rho(S; \mathbf{Q})$ follows from 'standard arguments' in Appendix B, the explicit form of $\rho$ is not provided in the main text before it is used to claim that biased methods increase curvature. comments.title: Ambiguity in 'Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection'
comments.quote: Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection. How to proceed in absence of the labels $y_i$ ? A natural idea would be to start from selection probabilities that use the labels... and then take a conditional expectation... We show that this is not always optimal.
comments.explanation: This claim is counter-intuitive. In Section 3, the authors explain that if you use a 'plug-in' fashion where you replace the optimal probability $\pi_0(y, x)$ with its expectation under the surrogate $E_{su}[\pi_0(y, x)|x]$, a more accurate surrogate (one closer to the true $P(y|x)$) might actually yield a higher generalization error. This is a subtle point. Usually, in decision theory, more information (a better model of the distribution) should lead to better outcomes. The authors are likely referring to the fact that the 'plug-in' strategy itself is suboptimal, and a better surrogate exacerbates the flaws of that specific strategy. Without seeing the explicit counter-example (likely in Section 6), this remains a 'questionable claim' in the sense that it sounds like a paradox. However, the authors qualify it with 'if used in a plugin fashion', which resolves the mathematical contradiction but leaves the methodological implication slightly ambiguous for practitioners.

comments.title: Possible score function mismatch in optimal non-reweighting scheme
comments.quote:
Specifically, the optimal $\pi_{\text{nr}}$ takes the form

$$\pi_{\text{nr}}(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbb{1}_{\{Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi_{\text{nr}}) \ge \lambda\}} + b(\mathbf{x}) \mathbb{1}_{\{Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi_{\text{nr}}) = \lambda\}}$$

where $\lambda$ and $b(\mathbf{x})$ are chosen so that $E \pi_{\text{nr}}(\mathbf{x}) = \gamma$. The resulting optimal asymptotic error is $\rho_{\text{nr}}(\mathbf{Q}) = \gamma^{-1} \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_{\pi_{\text{nr}}} \mathbf{H}_{\pi_{\text{nr}}}^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_{\pi_{\text{nr}}}^{-1})$
comments.explanation: In Proposition 4.3, the authors define $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi)$ in Eq (4.9) and subsequently claim that the optimal selection probability $\pi_{\text{nr}}$ is a threshold function of this score. However, there is a potential structural mismatch. Equation (4.9) defines $Z$ as a function of the *population* metrics $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})$ but weighted by the *selected* inverse Hessians $\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$. Typically, in these optimization problems (minimizing a trace of a matrix inverse under a linear constraint on $\pi$), the 'score' that determines the selection is the derivative of the objective function with respect to $\pi(\mathbf{x})$. While Eq (4.10) correctly identifies that the optimal $\pi$ should be a thresholding rule (a standard result from the Neyman-Pearson lemma logic in experimental design), the expression for $Z$ in (4.9) contains a leading negative sign: $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi) := - \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \dots) + 2 \text{Tr}(\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \dots)$. If $Z$ represents the 'value' or 'hardness' of a sample to be maximized, one would expect the components that increase the error (like the gradient covariance $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$) to have a positive coefficient in the selection score. If $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ (the noise) is high, the error $\rho$ increases; therefore, to minimize $\rho$, we usually want to avoid samples with high $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ unless they provide even higher curvature $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})$. The negative sign on the gradient term in (4.9) suggests that a high gradient covariance makes a sample *less* likely to be selected (if we select for $Z > \lambda$), which is logically consistent for noise reduction, but the paper later claims 'hard' examples (higher relative noise/influence) are preferred in low dimensions. There is a risk that (4.9) is the derivative of the error, meaning we should select for *minimal* $Z$, or that the signs are flipped relative to the 'hard vs easy' narrative in the text. This is a subtle point of derivation that might confuse a reader trying to implement the optimal $\pi$.

In Remark 4.1, the authors clarify that this is the conditional expectation of the *square* of the influence function's norm, specifically $E[ \| \psi \|^2 | \mathbf{x} ]^{1/2}$. However, the standard result for optimal subsampling in the unbiased (IAW) setting (e.g., Wang et al. 2018, 'Optimal Subsamping Methods for Generalized Linear Models') usually yields $\pi_i \propto \| \mathbf{x}_i \| |y_i - \hat{y}_i|$. When the label $y_i$ is unknown and we take an expectation over a surrogate, the 'optimal' approach under a fixed-budget constraint usually involves the $L_2$ norm of the influence function. If $Z(\mathbf{x})$ is defined as the expectation of the square (the second moment), then $Z(\mathbf{x})^{1/2}$ is correct. However, if the derivation intended to minimize total variance, one must be careful: the variance of the reweighted estimator involves $1/\pi_i$. The square root in (4.4) is consistent with the 'square root of the second moment' rule typically found in Ma et al. (2015), but it is sensitive to whether $Z$ was defined as the root or the square. The text in Remark 4.1 says 'take conditional expectation of the square... we compute $E[...] ^{1/2}$', which is internally consistent. The issue is that in some contexts of 'Influence functions,' authors use the expectation of the norm, not the square root of the expectation of the square. While mathematically sound as written, the distinction is non-trivial for the 'Standard' Influence Function claims made in the text.

However, Equation 6.1 is not present in the provided text or the summary. While the summary define $\pi_{nr}(\mathbf{x})$ based on a score function $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi)$, it does not explicitly define how $\alpha$ parameterizes this probability (e.g., as a power of the surrogate uncertainty or the $Z$ score). A reader cannot verify the claim that $\alpha = 1/2$ corresponds to influence-function based subsampling without the functional form of Eq. (6.1).

comments.title: Potential OCR Error in Surrogate Model Definition
comments.quote:
f :

su Here we use an ideal surrogate: \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \boldsymbol{\theta} 0.
comments.explanation: The text defining the surrogate model appears to have been corrupted during OCR or layout extraction. It says 'f : su Here we use an ideal surrogate: \hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \boldsymbol{\theta} 0.' This is syntactically broken. More importantly, the surrogate is previously defined as a probability kernel $P_{su}(dy|\mathbf{x})$. Stating the surrogate is an 'ideal surrogate' where $\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}} = \boldsymbol{\theta}_0$ (likely the ground truth parameters) confuses the surrogate model (a predictor used for selection) with the training outcome. It is unclear if the surrogate is the model $f(\mathbf{x}; \boldsymbol{\theta}_0)$ or if the authors are suggesting the selection was done using the true labels (which would contradict the 'weak supervision/no labels' premise of Section 2).

comments.title: Label Inconsistency in Synthetic Data Description
comments.explanation: In Figure 2's description and the text above it, the labels for the plots mention $P(Y=1|z) = f_1(z) = 0.1$ and similar values. It is mathematically ambiguous whether the authors mean $f_1(z)$ is a constant function equal to 0.1 (which would make $Y$ independent of $\mathbf{x}$ and the 'uncertainty' sampling logic moot) or if the value '0.1' refers to a different parameter like the noise level $\eta$. Given the text mentions $\eta = 0.95$ and $\zeta = 0.7$, the '0.1' in the figure header is likely a typo or a mislabeled parameter (perhaps the regularization $\lambda$ or a bias term), making the specific data-generating process for these 'misspecified models' impossible to reconstruct.

comments.title: Substrate Inconsistency in Definition of Z(x)
comments.quote: Z(\mathbf{x}) \propto E[\{ \| \psi(\mathbf{x}_i, y_i) \|^2_{\mathbf{Q}} | \mathbf{x}_i \}]^{1/2}
comments.explanation: In Section 4.1, Example 4.2 (Generalized Linear Models), specifically Equation (4.7), the text defines the sampling score $Z(\mathbf{x})$. However, the summary provided in the 'Key Equations' section defines the Optimal Unbiased Sampling Probability as $\pi_{unb}(\mathbf{x}) \propto \{ E[\|\psi(\mathbf{x}, y)\|^2_{\mathbf{Q}} | \mathbf{x}] \}^{1/2}$. Comparing this to Remark 4.1 in the text, there is a potential confusion between the score $Z(\mathbf{x})$ and the probability $\pi(\mathbf{x})$. Proposition 4.1 defines the optimal $\pi$ as being proportional to the square root of an expectation, which matches standard results in optimal subsampling (e.g., OSMAC). However, the notation $Z(\mathbf{x})$ is used in Equation (4.7) to represent the quantity inside the square root, while the high-dimensional section uses $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi)$ to represent a much more complex trace-based score for biased sampling. A reader might conflate the $Z$ in (4.7) with the $Z$ in (4.9). While likely an organizational choice to use $Z$ as a generic 'score' symbol, the lack of a distinct variable name for the influence-function norm specifically in the GLM context makes the transition to the biased score $Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi)$ in Section 4.2 harder to follow, especially since the definitions of 'hard' vs 'easy' samples rely on these scores.

comments.title: Potential Index Overlap in Asymptotic Error Coefficient
comments.quote: \rho(S; \mathbf{Q}) := \lim_{N \to \infty} N E[\|\hat{\boldsymbol{	heta}} - \boldsymbol{	heta}^*\|^2_{\mathbf{Q}}]
comments.explanation: The paper defines the error coefficient $\rho$ using a limit of $N E[\dots]$. In the low-dimensional regime (Section 4), $p$ is fixed and $n, N \to \infty$. Usually, the variance of an M-estimator scales as $1/n$ (the number of labeled samples used in training). Since $n = \gamma N$, the normalization $N$ is technically correct as it captures the $1/\gamma$ scaling. However, in Section 5 (High-dimensional regime), the paper introduces $\delta_0 = N/p$. If the error $\rho$ is meant to be a universal constant across both regimes, the transition from $N$ to $n$ in the influence function approximation ($\hat{\boldsymbol{	heta}} - \boldsymbol{	heta}^* \approx \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i \in G} w_i \psi_i$) suggests that for small $\gamma$, $\rho$ will blow up. The prose in Proposition 4.3 notes $\rho_{nr} = \gamma^{-1} Tr(\dots)$, which confirms the scaling. The potential issue is for a reader moving between Section 4 and Section 5: in high dimensions, the error typically scales with $p/n$ or $1/\delta$. The authors should be careful that $\rho$ remains defined relative to $N$ consistently, as the 'High-Dimensional Lagrangian' in the summary uses $\delta_0 = N/p$. If $\rho$ is the 'error coefficient', it usually refers to the constant $Tr(H^{-1} G H^{-1} Q)$, but here it includes the sampling ratio. This is a subtle point that might lead to a factor of $\gamma$ error when comparing results across regimes if not tracked closely.

comments.title: Unclear Definition of 'Misclassification Rate' in Surrogate Context
comments.quote: In this case $P_{su}(dy|\mathbf{x}) = P(dy|\mathbf{x})$ is the actual conditional distribution of the labels. Of course this is the very object we want to learn.
comments.explanation: The authors define the 'Ideal surrogate' as the true conditional distribution. In Definition 2.1 and the subsequent sections, they emphasize that selection probabilities $\pi_i$ depend on the surrogate. In Section 3, they claim that 'better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection.' While the mathematical results in the Appendix likely clarify this, the main text's prose creates a slight paradox: if the surrogate is 'Ideal' (the true posterior), and we use it to calculate $Z(\mathbf{x})$ (which depends on $E[\dots | \mathbf{x}]$), the surrogate is being used to find the most 'valuable' points. The claim that a 'weaker' surrogate can perform better (Figure 1 and Section 3) suggests that the bias introduced by an imperfect surrogate might coincidentally align with the optimal biased sampling rule (Proposition 4.3). However, the paper doesn't explicitly state whether the 'weak/strong' surrogates in Figure 1 are evaluated on their calibration or their rank-correlation with the true influence. Since the selection rule involves a threshold $\lambda$ that depends on the global distribution, a 'weak' surrogate that preserves the ranking of 'hard' vs 'easy' samples would be just as good as a 'strong' one. The claim 'Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection' is a strong one that requires the reader to distinguish between 'predictive accuracy of the surrogate' and 'utility of the surrogate for ranking samples.'

comments.title: Mathematical Error in Trace Derivation for Score Function Z
comments.quote: Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi) := - \text{Tr}\left( \mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{Q H}_{\pi}^{-1} \right) + 2\text{Tr}\left( \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{Q H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{G}_{\pi} \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \right)
comments.explanation: I attempted to derive the stationarity condition for minimizing the error $\rho(\pi) = \gamma^{-1} \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_\pi \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$ with respect to $\pi$, where $\mathbf{G}_\pi = \mathbb{E}[\pi(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})]/\mathbb{E}[\pi]$ and $\mathbf{H}_\pi = \mathbb{E}[\pi(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})]/\mathbb{E}[\pi]$. Using the matrix derivative identities $\frac{\partial}{\partial \pi} \mathbf{H}_\pi = \frac{1}{\mathbb{E}[\pi]} (\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) - \mathbf{H}_\pi)$ and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \pi} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} = -\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} (\frac{\partial \mathbf{H}_\pi}{\partial \pi}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$, the derivative of the trace term involves terms of the form $-\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_\pi \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$. While the second term in the paper's formula correctly features the $2 \text{Tr}(\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \dots)$ structure arising from the chain rule on the two inverse Hessians, the first term $-\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$ appears to have a sign error or be missing a component. Specifically, $\frac{\partial \mathbf{G}_\pi}{\partial \pi}$ should contribute a positive $\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$ to the score $Z$. A negative sign here would imply that increasing the gradient covariance (the 'noise') at a point makes that point *less* likely to be selected (since $\pi = \mathbb{1}(Z \ge \lambda)$), which contradicts the logic of upsampling 'hard'/high-variance examples in non-reweighted schemes.

comments.title: Definition of Optimal Asymptotic Error $\rho_{nr}$ Scalability
comments.quote: \rho_{nr}(\mathbf{Q}) = \gamma^{-1} \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_{\pi_{nr}} \mathbf{H}_{\pi_{nr}}^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_{\pi_{nr}}^{-1}) = \gamma^{-1} \inf_{\pi: \mathbb{E}[\pi]=\gamma} \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_{\pi} \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1})
comments.explanation: There appears to be an inconsistency in the scaling factor $\gamma^{-1}$. In Section 2, the authors define $\rho(S; \mathbf{Q})$ as the limit of $N \mathbb{E}[\dots]$. In the derivation of $\rho(S; \mathbf{Q})$ in Equation (B.5), the term is $\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_S \mathbf{H}_S^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_S^{-1})$. Since the non-reweighting scheme defines $\mathbf{G}_\pi$ as an expectation normalized by $\mathbb{E}[\pi] = \gamma$, the factor $\gamma^{-1}$ is already implicitly handled within the definition of $\mathbf{G}_\pi$ and $\mathbf{H}_\pi$ (both have a $1/\gamma$ factor, so $\mathbf{G}_\pi \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$ scales as $\gamma^{-1}$). Including an additional $\gamma^{-1}$ outside the Trace would result in a $\gamma^{-2}$ scaling, which is inconsistent with the standard variance scaling of $1/n$ ($1/(\gamma N)$) for M-estimators. I suspect either the Trace definition in (4.9)/(F.1) or the final expression for $\rho_{nr}$ has a redundant $\gamma^{-1}$ factor.

comments.title: OCR Error in Formula for $Z(x)$ in Example 4.2
comments.quote: Z(\mathbf{x}) \propto \{ \phi''(\langle \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x} \rangle)^2 + \mathbb{E}[ (y - \phi'(\langle \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x} \rangle))^2 | \mathbf{x} ] \phi''(\langle \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x} \rangle) \}^{1/2} \langle \mathbf{x}, \mathbf{H}^{-1} \mathbf{Q H}^{-1} \mathbf{x} \rangle^{1/2}
comments.explanation: In the context of GLMs, the influence function-based sampling probability typically depends on the square root of the variance of the score. The formula provided as (4.7) contains a term $(\phi'')^2 + \text{Var}(y|x)\phi''$. Under standard GLM properties (like the unity of the Fisher Information for the canonical link), we usually have $\phi''( \langle \theta, x \rangle ) = \text{Var}(y|x)$. If this identity holds, the term in the curly brackets simplifies to $2(\phi'')^2$. However, the OCR output $\mathbb{E}[ (y - \phi'(\langle \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x} \rangle))^2 | \mathbf{x} ] \phi''(\langle \boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{x} \rangle)$ suggests a product of two second-moment terms. Given the definition of $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ in (4.2) and the derivation in Prop 4.1, the intended term inside the curly brackets is likely the conditional variance of the loss gradient, which for GLMs is $\mathbb{E}[(y-\phi')^2|x] \mathbf{xx}^T$. The current expression is mathematically redundant or represents a misread of $(\mathbb{E}[(y-\phi')^2|x])^2$.

comments.title: Inconsistent Score Function Definition in Non-Reweighting Scheme
comments.quote: Z(\mathbf{x}; \pi) := - \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{QH}_{\pi}^{-1}) + 2\text{Tr}(\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{QH}_{\pi}^{-1} \mathbf{G}_{\pi} \mathbf{H}_{\pi}^{-1})
comments.explanation: I initially looked at the optimization problem to minimize $\rho(\pi, 1; \mathbf{Q}) = \gamma^{-1} \text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_\pi \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$. When deriving the first-order optimality condition (the gradient with respect to $\pi$), the derivative of $\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$ is $-\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x}) \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}$. Applying the product rule to $\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}_\pi \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1} \mathbf{Q} \mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$ should yield terms involving $\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})$ and $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})$. Specifically, the derivative with respect to $\pi$ (noting $\mathbf{G}_\pi$ and $\mathbf{H}_\pi$ are expectations normalized by $\gamma$) involves $\text{Tr}(\mathbf{G}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$ and $-2\text{Tr}(\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{x})\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1}\mathbf{G}_\pi\mathbf{H}_\pi^{-1})$. The formula in (4.9) has these two terms but with the signs reversed: it leads with a negative trace and follows with a positive $2 \text{Tr}$ term. Since the proposition states that $\pi_{nr}$ selects the *largest* score (hard thresholding $Z \ge \lambda$), the signs in the definition of $Z$ must be flipped for the thresholding rule to actually minimize the error. If $Z$ is defined as in (4.9), one would need to select samples with the *smallest* score to minimize the objective.

comments.title: Unspecified Normalization in Leverage Score Comparison
comments.quote: In Example 4.3... we recover a population version of the leverage score.
comments.explanation: The text states that in linear regression for prediction error ($\mathbf{Q} = \Sigma$), the score $Z(\mathbf{x})$ is proportional to $\langle \mathbf{x}, \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{x} \rangle$. I checked the standard definition of the population leverage score, which is indeed $\mathbf{x}^T \Sigma^{-1} \mathbf{x}$. However, in common subsampling literature (e.g., Ma et al. 2015), the optimal unbiased sampling probability for the *mean squared error* of parameters is often proportional to the square root of the leverage score if the noise is homoscedastic. The text mentions $Z$ is the square of the sampling probability in Remark 4.1 ($Z \propto \pi^2$). If $Z$ is the leverage score, then $\pi \propto \sqrt{\text{leverage}}$, which is correct for the influence function approach. However, if the authors mean the selection indicator $S_{nr}$ (biased) uses $Z$ as a hard threshold, the 'recovery' of leverage scores should clarify that it uses the leverage score as a threshold criterion rather than a probability, which changes the resulting sample distribution significantly compared to the cited literature.

comments.title: Potential Summation Index Error in Synthetic Selection Probability
comments.quote: We fix the constant $c(\gamma)$ so that $\sum_{i \le N} \pi(\mathbf{x}_i) = n$.
comments.explanation: In Section 6, the selection probability $\pi(\mathbf{x}_i)$ is defined using a surrogate model. The paper then states that $c(\gamma)$ is fixed so the sum of probabilities equals $n$. This is an empirical constraint on a specific realization of $\mathbf{X}$. However, the theoretical derivations (like Theorem 4 and Prop 4.1) rely on the expected sample size constraint: $\mathbb{E}[\pi(\mathbf{x})] = \gamma$. While for large $N$ the law of large numbers makes $\frac{1}{N} \sum \pi_i \approx \mathbb{E}[\pi]$, fixing $c$ based on the empirical sum makes the selection probabilities dependent across samples (as $c$ becomes a function of all $\mathbf{x}_i$), which technically violates the i.i.d. assumption used in the proofs. Unless the authors are using a Poisson sampling scheme where $c$ is determined by the population distribution (suggested by the 'Target Sample size constraint' in the Paper Summary), there's a slight misalignment between the simulation implementation and the theoretical framework.

progressive__glm-4.6.label: Progressive (glm-4.6)
progressive__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a significant and timely problem in machine learning, and the authors should be commended for the clarity and rigor of the initial problem formulation. The motivation for data selection under weak supervision is compelling, and the introduction does an excellent job of situating the work within the existing landscape, effectively distinguishing its focus on the `n = γN` asymptotic regime from prior studies on influence functions. However, the paper's potential impact is immediately undermined by a critical lack of clarity in presenting its initial results. The caption for Figure 1 is exceptionally dense and poorly structured, making it nearly impossible to parse the comparisons being made across different plots, surrogate models, and selection strategies. This issue is not merely cosmetic; it threatens to obscure the very empirical evidence that the paper uses to support its central, ambitious claims about the suboptimality of existing methods and the potential to outperform full-sample training. Before evaluating the substance of the theoretical and empirical contributions, a major revision is needed to ensure the results are communicated with the precision and clarity expected for a high-quality publication.

comments.title: Implicit definition in Proposition 4.3
comments.quote: Then there exists π_nr achieving the minimum of the asymptotic error over non-reweighting schemes ρ(π_nr, 1; Q) = inf_{S∈N} ρ(S; Q). Further, if H_{π_nr} ≻ 0 strictly, then π_nr takes the form π_nr(x) = 1{Z(x; π*) ≥ λ} · b(x) where λ and b(x) are chosen so that E π_nr(x) = γ.
comments.explanation: The optimal non-reweighting scheme π_nr is defined implicitly through Z(x; π*) = λ, where Z itself depends on π*. This circular definition makes the solution less transparent than the unbiased case. While mathematically correct (as the optimal solution depends on the selected data distribution), it could confuse readers expecting an explicit formula like in Proposition 4.1. The authors do provide intuition ('select the n examples with largest score') but the formal definition remains implicit.

comments.title: Strong claim in Theorem 1 without visible proof
comments.quote: For any M, there is a distribution of x_i and a γ ∈ (0,1) such that ρ_unb(Σ; γ)/ρ_nr(Σ; γ) > M.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 makes a strong claim that unbiased sampling can be arbitrarily worse than non-reweighting schemes. While this is an important result supporting the paper's main thesis, the proof is deferred to Appendix G which isn't shown in the provided context. Without seeing the proof, readers must accept this claim on faith. The theorem would be stronger if at least a sketch of the construction or key idea was provided in the main text.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'uncertainty-based subsampling'
comments.quote: Uncertainty-based subsampling is effective.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors state that 'uncertainty-based subsampling is effective' but don't precisely define what they mean by 'uncertainty-based' in this context. While they reference earlier research on uncertainty sampling and discuss surrogate models, the specific implementation of uncertainty-based subsampling in their framework isn't clearly specified. This could lead to ambiguity about which exact method they're referring to when making this claim.

comments.title: Connection between surrogate strength and selection effectiveness
comments.quote: Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection (if used in a plugin fashion as described).
comments.explanation: The authors make an interesting claim that better surrogate models don't always lead to better selection when used in a plugin fashion (taking conditional expectation under the surrogate). However, they don't provide much explanation or intuition for why this might be the case. This counterintuitive result deserves more discussion to help readers understand the underlying mechanism.

comments.title: Missing definitions for conditional gradient covariance and Hessian
comments.quote:
The conditional gradient covariance, and conditional Hessian will play an important role, and are defined below (recall that _**θ** ∗_ = arg min _**θ** R_ ( _**θ**_ )):

    [section 2] We consider weighted quadratic error _∥_ _**θ**_[ˆ] _−_ _**θ** ∗∥_[2] _**Q**_[:=] _[ ⟨]_ _**[θ]**_[ˆ] _[ −]_ _**[θ]**[∗][,]_ _**[ Q]**_[(ˆ] _**[θ]**[ −]_ _**[θ]**[∗]_[)] _[⟩]_[, where] _**[ Q]**[ ∈]_[R] _[p][×][p]_[.][This] covers both the standard _ℓ_ 2 estimation error (by setting _**Q**_ = _**I**_ ) and, under smoothness conditions, the test error _R_ test( _**θ**_[ˆ] ) (setting _**Q**_ = _∇_[2] _R_ test( _**θ** ∗_ )). We define the asymptotic error coefficient _ρ_ via
comments.explanation: The text states that the conditional gradient covariance and conditional Hessian will be defined, but these definitions are missing from the passage. Instead, the text jumps to defining the weighted quadratic error and asymptotic error coefficient. This appears to be either an OCR error or a missing part of the text. These definitions are crucial for understanding the subsequent analysis in Section 4.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about data subsampling beating full-sample training
comments.quote: **Data subsampling can beat full-sample training.** Our objective is to select a fraction _γ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) of the data such that, training on the selected subset yields test error close to training on the whole dataset. To our surprise, see Figure 1, we discover that good data selection can actually reduce the test error below the one obtained from the full sample for _γ_ as small as 0 _._ 4.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong claim that training on a subset (as small as 40% of the data) can outperform training on the full dataset. While they provide a brief explanation about information transfer from the surrogate model and reference Figure 1, this counterintuitive claim lacks sufficient theoretical justification in this section. The explanation that "information is being passed from the surrogate model to the trained model via data selection" is vague and doesn't fully explain how this could overcome the statistical disadvantage of using less data. This claim would benefit from more rigorous theoretical backing in the subsequent sections.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of unbiased data selection
comments.quote: **Definition 2.1.** _A data selection scheme is_ unbiased _if_ E _{S_ ( _**x**_ ) _|_ _**X** ,_ _**y** }_ = 1 _. We denote the set of unbiased data selection schemes by U ._
comments.explanation: The definition of unbiasedness uses the random variables S(x) introduced earlier, where S_i(x_i) = w(x_i) 1_{i∈G}. However, it's not immediately clear why E{S(x) | X, y} = 1 is the appropriate condition for unbiasedness. A more intuitive definition might relate to the unbiasedness of the resulting estimator or the empirical risk. The authors could clarify the connection between this condition and the traditional notion of unbiased estimation, especially since they later mention that with w_i = cst/π_i, the resulting empirical risk is "an unbiased version of the full empirical risk."

comments.title: Underspecified surrogate model
comments.quote: On the other hand, we will assume to have access to a surrogate model Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) which is a probability kernel from R _[d]_ to R. We will consider two settings – ( _i_ ) Ideal surrogate: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) = P(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is the actual conditional distribution of the labels. Of course this is the very object we want to learn. Since learning scheme is constrained, the problem is nevertheless non-trivial and informative. ( _ii_ ) Imperfect surrogates: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is predictive of the actual value of the labels, but does not coincide with the Bayes predictor.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the surrogate model as a key component but provides limited details about its practical implementation. While the two settings (ideal and imperfect) are described, there's no information about how these surrogates are obtained in practice, what architectures or methods are used, or how their quality is measured. This makes it difficult to assess the practical applicability of the proposed methods or to understand the relationship between surrogate quality and selection effectiveness (though Section 3 does note that "better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection").

comments.title: Unjustified claim in Remark 4.1
comments.quote: Interestingly, the optimal way to deal with unknown yi is to take conditional expectation of the square of the sampling probability: we compute E{∥ψ(x_i, y_i)∥_Q | x_i}^{1/2} instead of E{∥ψ(x_i, y_i)∥_Q | x_i}.
comments.explanation: The authors claim it's optimal to take the square root inside the conditional expectation rather than outside. This is a non-trivial mathematical claim that affects how influence functions are used in practice. While they reference Appendix C for details, the significance of this choice deserves at least a brief intuition in the main text. Without this, readers might wonder whether this is a typo or a key insight.

comments.title: Weak justification for GLM parameterization
comments.quote: P(y|x) = exp(y⟨θ*, x⟩ - φ(⟨θ*, x⟩)) ν₀(dy)
comments.explanation: In Example 4.2, the authors present a parameterization for generalized linear models that differs from the standard formulation. While mathematically equivalent, the connection to the usual GLM exponential family form isn't immediately clear. A brief note explaining that ν₀ is the base measure and how this relates to the standard GLM parameterization would help readers familiar with the conventional formulation.

comments.title: Insufficient justification for counterintuitive result
comments.quote: Theorem 2. _Assume_ _**x** i ∼_ N(0 _,_ _**I** d_ ) _, and yi distributed according to Eq._ (4.8) _. Let_ _**Q**_ = _**H**_ := _∇_[2] _R_ ( _**θ** ∗_ ) _. Then both for linear and logistic regression there exists_ P( _A|t_ ) _such that γ → ρ_ nr( _**Q**_ ; _γ_ ) _is monotone increasing in a non-empty interval_ ( _γ_ 0 _,_ 1) _._
comments.explanation: Theorem 2 presents a counterintuitive result - that the error can increase as more data is used (non-monotonic behavior). While the theorem references Appendix I for proof, the statement lacks any intuition or explanation for why this phenomenon occurs. Given how surprising this result is (contrary to the usual monotonic decrease in error with more data), even a brief explanation of the mechanism would help readers understand and accept this finding.

comments.title: Ambiguous selection scheme in Section 6
comments.quote: We then perform data selection, with selection probability π( _**x** i_ ) ∝ p(⟨ _**θ**_[ˆ] su _,_ _**x** i⟩_ )^α
comments.explanation: The selection probability depends on parameter α, but the paper doesn't specify what values of α are considered or how α relates to selecting 'hard' vs 'easy' examples. Since this is a key parameter controlling the selection strategy, readers need clarification on whether α > 1 biases toward harder examples, what range of α values were tested, and how α was chosen in the experiments.

comments.title: Underspecified experimental setup in Section 6
comments.quote: In this section we present numerical simulations within the synthetic data model introduced in Section 5.1, for binary labels _yi ∈{_ +1 _, −_ 1 _}_ .
comments.explanation: The numerical experiments section lacks key methodological details needed for reproducibility: (1) The dimension p used in simulations, (2) The range of subsampling fractions γ tested, (3) How the ridge parameter λ is selected, (4) The specific form of the link function f(⟨θ_0, x_i⟩) for generating labels. Without these details, readers cannot fully understand or reproduce the experimental results.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation in misspecified model definition
comments.quote: We consider two misspecified models defined by (here _η_ = 0 _._ 95 = 1 _− η_ , _ζ_ = 0 _._ 7 = 1 _− ζ_[¯] ): _f_ :
comments.explanation: The notation defining the misspecified models contains a mathematical inconsistency. The text states '_η_ = 0 _._ 95 = 1 _− η_' which would imply η = 0.95 = 0.05, which is impossible. Similarly, '_ζ_ = 0 _._ 7 = 1 _− ζ_[¯]_' would imply ζ = 0.7 = 0.3. This appears to be an OCR error where the authors likely meant to define both η and its complement η̄ = 1 - η, but the notation got garbled. A careful reader would be confused by this contradiction. The most plausible intended meaning is probably η = 0.95 (so η̄ = 0.05) and ζ = 0.7 (so ζ̄ = 0.3), but as written, this is mathematically incorrect.

comments.title: Incomplete definition in Assumption A2
comments.quote: Further, for every u ∈ S_{p-1}, define L∞(u; y, x) ∈ [0, ∞]
comments.explanation: The definition of L∞ is incomplete. It specifies the codomain [0, ∞] but doesn't define the actual function. This makes Assumption A2 unclear - we don't know what property L∞ is supposed to satisfy. The authors likely meant to define a supremum or limit condition, but as written, the assumption is incomplete.

comments.title: Unjustified reparameterization in proof
comments.quote: For ∥ξ∥ < 1, define θ(ξ) = c(∥ξ∥) ξ, where c(r) = 1/(1−r²)
comments.explanation: The choice of this specific reparameterization with c(r) = 1/(1−r²) is not justified. While it appears to be a technical device to ensure the minimizer stays within the unit ball, the authors don't explain why this particular form is needed or why simpler approaches wouldn't work. The function c(r) diverges as r→1, which could cause issues that aren't addressed.

comments.title: Missing verification of theorem conditions
comments.quote: by van der Vaart (2000, Theorem 5.13), we have ξ̂ → ξ∗ almost surely... Using van der Vaart (2000, Theorem 5.14) (whose assumptions follow from A3, A4), we get...
comments.explanation: The authors claim that the conditions of these theorems follow from A3 and A4, but don't provide the verification. This is a non-trivial step since van der Vaart's theorems have specific technical requirements (e.g., Donsker conditions, entropy conditions) that aren't obviously satisfied by the given assumptions. The reader is left to trust this claim without evidence.

comments.title: Notation H̃_w,π,Q appears without definition
comments.quote: where H̃_w,π,Q := H_π − G_π H_π^−1 Q H_π^−1 G_π
comments.explanation: This notation appears suddenly in the proof of Lemma E.2 without prior definition. While it's clear from context that it's related to the H_S matrix from Proposition B.1, the exact relationship and meaning aren't explained. This makes it hard to follow the derivation of the inequality.

comments.title: Strong claim without justification in Remark B.1
comments.quote: If it does not hold, then the resulting error ∥θ̂ − θ∗∥_Q^2 will be, in general, of order one.
comments.explanation: This claim about the error being "of order one" if A1 doesn't hold seems strong without further justification. The authors don't specify what "order one" means precisely (O(1) or O_P(1)?) or under what conditions this holds. The error could potentially diverge or depend on the degree of misspecification.

progressive_original__glm-4.6.label: Progressive Original (glm-4.6)
progressive_original__glm-4.6.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a significant and timely problem in machine learning, and the authors should be commended for the clarity and rigor of the initial problem formulation. The motivation for data selection under weak supervision is compelling, and the introduction does an excellent job of situating the work within the existing landscape, effectively distinguishing its focus on the `n = γN` asymptotic regime from prior studies on influence functions. However, the paper's potential impact is immediately undermined by a critical lack of clarity in presenting its initial results. The caption for Figure 1 is exceptionally dense and poorly structured, making it nearly impossible to parse the comparisons being made across different plots, surrogate models, and selection strategies. This issue is not merely cosmetic; it threatens to obscure the very empirical evidence that the paper uses to support its central, ambitious claims about the suboptimality of existing methods and the potential to outperform full-sample training. Before evaluating the substance of the theoretical and empirical contributions, a major revision is needed to ensure the results are communicated with the precision and clarity expected for a high-quality publication.

comments.title: Implicit definition in Proposition 4.3
comments.quote: Then there exists π_nr achieving the minimum of the asymptotic error over non-reweighting schemes ρ(π_nr, 1; Q) = inf_{S∈N} ρ(S; Q). Further, if H_{π_nr} ≻ 0 strictly, then π_nr takes the form π_nr(x) = 1{Z(x; π*) ≥ λ} · b(x) where λ and b(x) are chosen so that E π_nr(x) = γ.
comments.explanation: The optimal non-reweighting scheme π_nr is defined implicitly through Z(x; π*) = λ, where Z itself depends on π*. This circular definition makes the solution less transparent than the unbiased case. While mathematically correct (as the optimal solution depends on the selected data distribution), it could confuse readers expecting an explicit formula like in Proposition 4.1. The authors do provide intuition ('select the n examples with largest score') but the formal definition remains implicit.

comments.title: Strong claim in Theorem 1 without visible proof
comments.quote: For any M, there is a distribution of x_i and a γ ∈ (0,1) such that ρ_unb(Σ; γ)/ρ_nr(Σ; γ) > M.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 makes a strong claim that unbiased sampling can be arbitrarily worse than non-reweighting schemes. While this is an important result supporting the paper's main thesis, the proof is deferred to Appendix G which isn't shown in the provided context. Without seeing the proof, readers must accept this claim on faith. The theorem would be stronger if at least a sketch of the construction or key idea was provided in the main text.

comments.title: Ambiguous reference to 'uncertainty-based subsampling'
comments.quote: Uncertainty-based subsampling is effective.
comments.explanation: In Section 3, the authors state that 'uncertainty-based subsampling is effective' but don't precisely define what they mean by 'uncertainty-based' in this context. While they reference earlier research on uncertainty sampling and discuss surrogate models, the specific implementation of uncertainty-based subsampling in their framework isn't clearly specified. This could lead to ambiguity about which exact method they're referring to when making this claim.

comments.title: Connection between surrogate strength and selection effectiveness
comments.quote: Better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection (if used in a plugin fashion as described).
comments.explanation: The authors make an interesting claim that better surrogate models don't always lead to better selection when used in a plugin fashion (taking conditional expectation under the surrogate). However, they don't provide much explanation or intuition for why this might be the case. This counterintuitive result deserves more discussion to help readers understand the underlying mechanism.

comments.title: Missing definitions for conditional gradient covariance and Hessian
comments.quote:
The conditional gradient covariance, and conditional Hessian will play an important role, and are defined below (recall that _**θ** ∗_ = arg min _**θ** R_ ( _**θ**_ )):

    [section 2] We consider weighted quadratic error _∥_ _**θ**_[ˆ] _−_ _**θ** ∗∥_[2] _**Q**_[:=] _[ ⟨]_ _**[θ]**_[ˆ] _[ −]_ _**[θ]**[∗][,]_ _**[ Q]**_[(ˆ] _**[θ]**[ −]_ _**[θ]**[∗]_[)] _[⟩]_[, where] _**[ Q]**[ ∈]_[R] _[p][×][p]_[.][This] covers both the standard _ℓ_ 2 estimation error (by setting _**Q**_ = _**I**_ ) and, under smoothness conditions, the test error _R_ test( _**θ**_[ˆ] ) (setting _**Q**_ = _∇_[2] _R_ test( _**θ** ∗_ )). We define the asymptotic error coefficient _ρ_ via
comments.explanation: The text states that the conditional gradient covariance and conditional Hessian will be defined, but these definitions are missing from the passage. Instead, the text jumps to defining the weighted quadratic error and asymptotic error coefficient. This appears to be either an OCR error or a missing part of the text. These definitions are crucial for understanding the subsequent analysis in Section 4.

comments.title: Unjustified claim about data subsampling beating full-sample training
comments.quote: **Data subsampling can beat full-sample training.** Our objective is to select a fraction _γ ∈_ (0 _,_ 1) of the data such that, training on the selected subset yields test error close to training on the whole dataset. To our surprise, see Figure 1, we discover that good data selection can actually reduce the test error below the one obtained from the full sample for _γ_ as small as 0 _._ 4.
comments.explanation: The authors make a strong claim that training on a subset (as small as 40% of the data) can outperform training on the full dataset. While they provide a brief explanation about information transfer from the surrogate model and reference Figure 1, this counterintuitive claim lacks sufficient theoretical justification in this section. The explanation that "information is being passed from the surrogate model to the trained model via data selection" is vague and doesn't fully explain how this could overcome the statistical disadvantage of using less data. This claim would benefit from more rigorous theoretical backing in the subsequent sections.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of unbiased data selection
comments.quote: **Definition 2.1.** _A data selection scheme is_ unbiased _if_ E _{S_ ( _**x**_ ) _|_ _**X** ,_ _**y** }_ = 1 _. We denote the set of unbiased data selection schemes by U ._
comments.explanation: The definition of unbiasedness uses the random variables S(x) introduced earlier, where S_i(x_i) = w(x_i) 1_{i∈G}. However, it's not immediately clear why E{S(x) | X, y} = 1 is the appropriate condition for unbiasedness. A more intuitive definition might relate to the unbiasedness of the resulting estimator or the empirical risk. The authors could clarify the connection between this condition and the traditional notion of unbiased estimation, especially since they later mention that with w_i = cst/π_i, the resulting empirical risk is "an unbiased version of the full empirical risk."

comments.title: Underspecified surrogate model
comments.quote: On the other hand, we will assume to have access to a surrogate model Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) which is a probability kernel from R _[d]_ to R. We will consider two settings – ( _i_ ) Ideal surrogate: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) = P(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is the actual conditional distribution of the labels. Of course this is the very object we want to learn. Since learning scheme is constrained, the problem is nevertheless non-trivial and informative. ( _ii_ ) Imperfect surrogates: In this case Psu(d _y|_ _**x**_ ) is predictive of the actual value of the labels, but does not coincide with the Bayes predictor.
comments.explanation: The paper introduces the surrogate model as a key component but provides limited details about its practical implementation. While the two settings (ideal and imperfect) are described, there's no information about how these surrogates are obtained in practice, what architectures or methods are used, or how their quality is measured. This makes it difficult to assess the practical applicability of the proposed methods or to understand the relationship between surrogate quality and selection effectiveness (though Section 3 does note that "better surrogate models do not always lead to better selection").

comments.title: Implicit definition in Proposition 4.3

While mathematically valid, this creates a circular definition that could confuse readers. The authors don't clarify whether this defines a unique solution or how it would be computed in practice. Given that this is a key theoretical result, a brief note on the existence/uniqueness of the fixed point would strengthen the presentation.

comments.title: Unjustified claim in Remark 4.1

While they reference Appendix C for details, the significance of this choice deserves at least a brief intuition in the main text. Without this, readers might wonder whether this is a typo or a key insight.

A brief note explaining that ν₀ is the base measure and how this relates to the standard GLM parameterization would help readers familiar with the conventional formulation.

In mathematical notation, this should be 'γ ↦' to denote a function mapping, or more clearly stated as 'the function γ → ρ_nr(Q; γ) is monotone increasing'. While readers can likely infer the intended meaning, this notation inconsistency could cause confusion.

comments.explanation: There's a grammatical error in 'with _n_ su + _n_ is significantly below _N_'. This should read 'with _n_ su + _n_ significantly below _N_' or 'where _n_ su + _n_ is significantly below _N_'. While the meaning is clear from context, this grammatical construction is incorrect.

comments.quote: Theorem 2. _Assume_ _**x** i ∼_ N(0 _,_ _**I** d_ ) _, and yi distributed according to Eq._ (4.8) _. Let_ _**Q**_ = _**H**_ := _∇_[2] _R_ ( _**θ** ∗_ ) _.
